us-politics.headline: Iran Downs US Jets, World Leaders Move Without Trump
us-politics.body:
Iran shot down American fighter jets, triggering sharp criticism at home and diplomatic scrambling abroad. Rep. Seth Moulton declared Iran is winning the war, while former National Security Advisor John Bolton suggested Trump is in panic mode. The incidents mark a serious escalation in an already volatile standoff.

World leaders are not waiting for Washington to lead. Multiple governments are coordinating their own response to the Strait of Hormuz crisis, effectively bypassing the Trump administration. That signals a broader erosion of US credibility in the region, at least in the short term.

At home, the political fallout is immediate. Critics on the left argue the administration lacks a coherent Iran strategy. Supporters counter that military confrontations under any president carry risk, and that strength requires resolve.

sources.title: Moulton says ‘Iran is winning the war’ after US jets downed
Bolton: Trump likely in ‘panic mode’ after Iran attacks US fighter jets
World leaders bypass Trump to tackle Strait of Hormuz crisis

us-politics.headline: Cabinet Shake-Ups and SCOTUS Birthright Case Rattle Washington
us-politics.body:
The departures of Pam Bondi and Kristi Noem from the Cabinet are fuelling speculation about further shake-ups inside the Trump administration. Both exits were abrupt, and analysts across the spectrum are questioning who is next and why the churn continues at senior levels.

Meanwhile, the Supreme Court is weighing birthright citizenship, one of the most consequential constitutional questions in years. NPR's political roundup noted the case could reshape immigration law fundamentally. Conservatives argue the 14th Amendment has been misread for decades. Civil rights advocates warn any rollback would strip citizenship from thousands of American-born children.

Separately, a child of Chinese illegal immigrants was charged with planting an explosive at a US military base, a case Fox News highlighted as evidence of national security risks tied to border policy. Critics of that framing say individual crimes should not define broader immigration debates.

sources.title: Week in Politics: Trump's latest speech; SCOTUS on birthright citizenship; Pam Bondi
Bondi, Noem ousters raise questions about future Cabinet shake-ups
Child of Chinese illegal immigrants charged with planting explosive at US military base

us-politics.headline: Trump Pushes States on Day Care, Signs College Sports Order
us-politics.body:
President Trump floated shifting federal day care policy to the states over the Easter weekend, drawing swift pushback from child care advocates who called the idea unworkable. Costs vary enormously by state, and critics argue a devolved system would deepen inequality for working families. Supporters say states are better placed than Washington to tailor solutions.

Trump also signed an executive order targeting college sports reform, aiming at the NCAA's governance structure. Details remain thin, but the move follows years of bipartisan frustration over athlete compensation and transfer rules. Whether executive action can meaningfully reshape a private governing body is an open legal question.

On Good Friday, Trump issued a message declaring America needs God and touting a resurgence of religion, a signal to his evangelical base ahead of what his team frames as a culturally significant moment.

sources.title: Trump floats letting states handle day care. Advocates call it a lead balloon
Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'

us-politics.headline: Alcatraz Budget, Wyoming Race, and Retirement Savings Slide
us-politics.body:
The Trump administration included $152 million in its budget request to reopen Alcatraz as a high-security federal prison, following through on a proposal many dismissed as symbolic when it was first floated. The request makes it a concrete policy ask, though Congress controls the purse strings and the idea faces skepticism from fiscal hawks and criminal justice reformers alike.

In Wyoming, the son of a Republican megadonor entered the race for the state's open at-large House seat, adding a well-funded name to a competitive primary. The seat is safe Republican territory, so the primary is effectively the election.

On the economic front, a payroll firm's new data shows Americans are saving less for retirement, a trend that cuts across political lines but lands hardest on lower-income workers. Economists warn the erosion could create long-term fiscal pressure regardless of which party holds power.

sources.title: Alcatraz could reopen as a 'state-of-the-art secure prison' under Trump's $152M budget request
Son of Republican megadonor throws hat in the ring for open at-large House seat in Wyoming
Warning sign? Retirement saving habits eroding, payroll firm says

au-politics.headline: Pezzullo Report Reveals 'Reckless' Ties to Liberal Powerbroker
au-politics.body:
A confidential report has found former Home Affairs secretary Michael Pezzullo acted recklessly in his dealings with a Liberal Party powerbroker, adding fresh detail to the conduct that led to his sacking in 2023. The findings raise ongoing questions about the boundary between senior public servants and political operatives.

Pezzullo was dismissed after text messages revealed years of communication with Liberal factional figure Scott Briggs. The confidential report's language - 'reckless' rather than deliberate - may shape how accountability measures are applied, though critics argue the distinction offers little comfort given the seniority of the role involved.

sources.title: Confidential report found former home affairs boss Michael Pezzullo was ‘reckless’ in engagement with Liberal powerbroker

au-politics.headline: Murder Charges, Missing Persons Cases Dominate Crime Headlines
au-politics.body:
Victorian police arrested two people in connection with the disappearance of Dezi Freeman, while a separate man was charged with murder after being intercepted attempting to board an international flight - a case linked to a missing woman near Little River. A third murder charge was laid against a woman in Laverton following the death of a 24-year-old.

In Western Australia, a full-scale search is underway for a woman missing at The Gap near Albany, a popular but hazardous tourism site. The cluster of cases reflects active law enforcement pressure across multiple states, with several investigations still unresolved.

sources.title: Man trying to board international flight charged with murder
Victoria police arrest two people as part of Dezi Freeman investigation
Police launch full-scale search to locate woman missing in popular tourism spot
Two arrested as part of investigation into movements of Dezi Freeman
Woman charged with murder following investigation into death of 24yo

au-politics.headline: Fuel Fears Ease, But Farmers and Councils Feel the Squeeze
au-politics.body:
Energy Minister Chris Bowen says fuel supply has stabilised, with fewer service stations running dry after a spike in panic buying. Despite the reassurance, pressure on the supply chain has not fully eased.

Farmers are calling on supermarkets to absorb higher transport costs by paying more for produce, warning that fuel price volatility threatens margins already under strain. Separately, Sydney councils are pushing back against a wave of new data centres, citing risks of localised blackouts, reduced housing capacity, and health concerns for nearby residents - a conflict that pits infrastructure demand against community planning priorities.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Just, you know, check your tyre pressure
Supermarkets asked to pay more for produce to absorb fuel costs for farmers
Sydney councils fear new datacentres could cause blackouts, block housing and affect locals’ health

au-politics.headline: Victorian Election Takes Shape as Challenger Targets Premier Allan
au-politics.body:
Andrew Lethlean has confirmed he will contest Bendigo East against Victorian Premier Jacinta Allan at the next state election, setting up a direct challenge to the Labor leader in her own seat. Incumbents rarely lose their own electorates, but the move signals opposition intent to stretch Labor's resources and put Allan on the defensive at home.

The contest adds a personal dimension to what is shaping up as a competitive Victorian election cycle. Allan has faced sustained criticism over cost-of-living pressures and infrastructure project costs, and a credible local challenger could complicate her ability to campaign freely across the state.

sources.title: Lethlean to challenge Victorian premier in Bendigo East

world.headline: US-Israeli War on Iran Enters Day 36 With Escalating Strikes
world.body:
US and Israeli forces continued strikes against Iran on day 36 of the conflict, with Iranian missiles hitting residential areas in central Israel in retaliation. A projectile struck near Iran's Bushehr nuclear plant, killing one person - the IAEA confirmed the incident and raised alarm over risks to nuclear infrastructure.

A US airman is missing inside Iran after a military incident, with rescue teams describing the search as harrowing and dangerous. Iran simultaneously executed two members of a banned opposition group, a move critics linked to the wartime security crackdown. The conflict is reshaping the region's military and political landscape at speed.

sources.title: Projectile hits near Iran’s Bushehr nuclear plant, killing one: IAEA
What we know so far about the search for missing US airman in Iran
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran executes two convicted members of banned opposition group
Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?
Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel

world.headline: Russia Escalates Over Easter as Ukraine Death Toll Rises
world.body:
Russia launched fresh attacks on Ukraine over the Easter period, killing at least five people in a strike on a market and at least four others in separate assaults. Zelensky accused Moscow of choosing escalation over a ceasefire, saying Russia deliberately timed the attacks to coincide with the holiday.

The strikes underscore Russia's rejection of repeated calls for a pause in fighting. Ukraine has framed the timing as a deliberate provocation aimed at civilian morale.

sources.title: Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five
At least four people killed in Russian attacks on Ukraine
Russia chose 'Easter escalation' over ceasefire, says Zelensky

world.headline: Israeli Ground Forces Advance in Lebanon, Hospital Struck in Tyre
world.body:
Israeli ground forces pushed further into Lebanon as strikes damaged a hospital in the southern city of Tyre, raising fresh concerns about attacks on protected medical facilities. The advance marks a significant expansion of Israeli military operations beyond Gaza.

Critics, including European Parliament member Rima Hassan, accused Israel's allies of enabling unchecked military action. The hospital strike drew condemnation from humanitarian groups monitoring the conflict.

sources.title: Israeli strikes damage hospital in Lebanon’s Tyre; ground invasion advances
Israel’s unchecked war machine
European Parliament’s Rima Hassan: I’m a target of ‘political harassment’

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Halfway to Moon, Sends Back Earth Images
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew reached the halfway point of their lunar flyby mission, releasing striking images of Earth taken from deep space. The photographs, described by the crew as spectacular, are the first taken by humans from that distance in decades.

The mission marks a major milestone in NASA's push to return humans to the Moon. No landing is planned for this flight - it is a crewed test of the Orion spacecraft's systems ahead of future surface missions.

sources.title: Artemis II crew now halfway to Moon as they take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA unveils first images of Earth from Artemis II

ai.headline: Anthropic's Busy Week: Biotech Deal, PAC, and Claude Controversies
ai.body:
Anthropic is moving fast on multiple fronts. The company acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a political action committee to expand its Washington presence, and is riding high in private markets - though SpaceX's anticipated IPO may soon compete for the same investor dollars.

Not everything went smoothly. Anthropic effectively shut out OpenClaw users from Claude subscriptions, forcing them to pay extra - a move that drew sharp criticism from developers. Separately, hackers exploited a Claude Code source leak to distribute malware, and security researchers are urging OpenClaw users to assume their systems are compromised. Claude Code also made headlines for a better reason: it independently uncovered a Linux vulnerability that had gone undetected for 23 years.

sources.title: Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Leadership as Key Executives Step Back
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new "special projects" role, while Fidji Simo is taking medical leave. The company's AGI lead is also stepping away temporarily - three senior departures or shifts in quick succession.

The timing is notable. OpenAI is under pressure to convert to a for-profit structure and close new funding rounds. Leadership instability at this stage raises questions about internal cohesion, even as the company pushes forward on frontier model development.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: AI Is Eroding Critical Thinking, and Musk Wants Grok Fees From Banks
ai.body:
New research finds that heavy AI users are increasingly willing to outsource their reasoning entirely to large language models - a pattern researchers call "cognitive surrender." The finding lands as AI tools embed deeper into professional workflows.

Meanwhile, Elon Musk is demanding that banks working on the SpaceX IPO purchase Grok subscriptions, blurring the line between business leverage and product promotion. Trump's AI data center push is also stumbling - his own tariffs are driving up hardware costs and slowing construction, undermining the initiative's core goals.

sources.title: Elon Musk insists banks working on SpaceX IPO must buy Grok subscriptions
"Cognitive surrender" leads AI users to abandon logical thinking, research finds
Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing

ai.headline: Artemis II Heads to the Moon as NASA Budget Faces Cuts
ai.body:
Artemis II is en route to the Moon and early reports say the mission is running smoothly - a genuine milestone for NASA's crewed lunar program. The timing is awkward. The Trump administration has simultaneously proposed steep cuts to NASA's budget, raising doubts about funding for future Artemis missions even as this one unfolds.

The contradiction captures a broader tension in US space policy: ambitious goals paired with shrinking institutional support.

sources.title: Trump proposes steep cut to NASA budget as astronauts head for the Moon
As Artemis II zooms to the Moon, everything seems to be going swimmingly

btc.headline: Bitcoin Bears Face $2.5B Wipeout as Market Thins
btc.body:
Short sellers are sitting on dangerous ground. A move to $72,000 would trigger $2.5 billion in liquidations, and analysts warn that Bitcoin's directionless consolidation often precedes a sharp, decisive break rather than a slow grind.

The setup looks fragile beneath the surface. Five separate data sources point to the same problem: liquidity is quietly draining from the Bitcoin market, meaning any sudden move will hit harder than usual with fewer orders to absorb it. Large traders felt that pain all quarter - wealthy Bitcoin holders lost an average of $337 million per day through Q1 2026.

Despite the carnage, some analysts stay bullish long-term. Bitcoin ETFs are projected to eventually surpass gold ETFs in total size, suggesting institutional appetite remains intact even as short-term traders bleed.

sources.title: Bitcoin shorts risk $2.5 billion liquidation at $72K: Are bears in danger?
Five data sources say the same thing about bitcoin market. It's thinning from the inside
Rich Bitcoin traders lost $337M daily in first quarter of 2026
Bitcoin’s ‘no direction’ action may lead to heavier breakout: Analyst
Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst

btc.headline: Quantum Threat Looms, But Bitcoin's Fix Has Costs
btc.body:
Quantum computing's threat to Bitcoin is real but widely misunderstood. Recent headlines about cracking Bitcoin in nine minutes describe theoretical attacks on specific cryptographic keys, not a live capability - but the underlying vulnerability is genuine and the industry knows it.

Solana's attempt to prepare for a post-quantum world illustrates the problem clearly. Quantum-resistant encryption algorithms are significantly heavier than current ones, forcing a direct tradeoff between security and the high transaction speeds that make Solana competitive. There is no free upgrade here.

The pressure is building across the industry to act before quantum hardware catches up with the threat models. How networks balance that tradeoff will shape which blockchains survive the next decade of cryptographic risk.

sources.title: Solana's quantum-threat readiness reveals harsh tradeoff: security vs speed
Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

btc.headline: Prediction Markets Hit Legal Walls in Asia and Nevada
btc.body:
Prediction markets are running into regulators on multiple fronts. A Nevada judge extended a ban on Kalshi's sports betting markets, blocking the platform from operating in the state while legal arguments over federal jurisdiction versus state gambling law continue to grind through the courts.

In Asia, platforms are testing the limits of strict local regulations, probing where financial prediction markets end and illegal gambling begins - a line that varies sharply by country and remains largely unresolved.

Polymarket added its own controversy by pulling a market tied to a missing US pilot after public backlash, highlighting the reputational risks that come with allowing markets on sensitive real-world events. The legal and ethical boundaries of prediction markets are being drawn in real time.

sources.title: Prediction markets are testing legal limits in strict Asian markets
Polymarket takes down market on missing US pilot after backlash
Judge continues Nevada ban on Kalshi sports markets

btc.headline: Tether Eyes $500B Valuation but Investors May Balk
btc.body:
Tether is weighing a fundraising round that would value the company at $500 billion, but the stablecoin giant may shelve the plan if investor demand falls short of that target. The figure is striking given that Tether's core business - issuing USDT - generates enormous revenue from US Treasury holdings, but a $500 billion valuation would place it among the most valuable financial companies on earth.

The hesitation signals that even Tether recognizes the number is a stretch. Institutional investors willing to write large checks at that price may simply not exist in sufficient numbers right now.

sources.title: Tether may delay fundraising if demand falls short at $500B valuation: Report

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, buttery cod fillets marinated in white miso, mirin, and sake then broiled until caramelised and lacquered
food.tip: Marinate for at least 24 hours — the longer the fish sits, the deeper the umami penetration and the better the glaze caramelises under the broiler

exercise.name: The Saturday Deck of Cards
exercise.equipment: One standard deck of playing cards, a mat
exercise.description: Assign a bodyweight move to each suit — e.g. push-ups, squats, burpees, hollow-body holds — then flip cards one by one and perform the number of reps shown, turning a random deck into an unpredictable full-body circuit

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to the human body, whose cells replace themselves over years, it becomes a sharp question about what 'you' actually are across time
learn.why: On a quiet Saturday it's a genuinely destabilising rabbit hole: start with the Greek myth, follow it through Hobbes and Locke, then land on Derek Parfit's modern work on personal identity — by the end you'll question whether the person who wakes up tomorrow is meaningfully the same one reading this now
